title: The Truth Feels Better
lesson: Telling the truth after a mistake mends things and makes you feel lighter inside.
prompts: In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf holds a plain glossy blue ceramic flower vase, and the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands on the wooden floor, one arm raised mid-throw having just tossed a red ball up toward the vase, the red ball in mid-air between their open hand and the vase arcing straight at it, with a playful expression. Medium-wide shot at eye level, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame) — with a little spilled water pooling around them; the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands among the broken fragments, both hands flying up beside their cheeks with fingers spread and eyes wide in shock. Medium shot at eye level, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame); the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands alone beside the broken fragments, one hand pressed to their cheek, looking down worriedly. Eye-level medium shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame); the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands, with an honest, slightly nervous expression, one arm extended low and the index finger clearly pointing down at the broken fragments on the floor. Nearby, {GENDER_F_AGE_35_PARENT} kneels down to their level, listening with a calm, understanding look. Make the interaction read physically: each person faces the other person or the shared object, with hands and gaze directed to one connected action. If a prop is shared, show one shared prop linking their hands in one common space. Eye-level medium-wide shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the wooden shelf is completely bare with nothing standing on it, and the blue ceramic vase lies smashed to pieces on the wooden floor — broken into thin-walled, curved, jagged-edged blue pottery fragments of various sizes, all lying scattered on the floor with no piece whole or upright (no intact or standing vase anywhere in frame); the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image kneels beside the fragments, holding a small hand broom and carefully sweeping the jagged pieces toward one open dustpan on the wooden floor, with a calmer, responsible expression. {GENDER_F_AGE_35_PARENT} kneels nearby, holding the dustpan steady by its handle and smiling gently as she helps guide the cleanup. Make the cleanup read physically connected: both people face the dustpan and broken fragments, with hands and gaze directed to one shared dustpan-and-broom action. Eye-level medium-wide shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}

In a cozy living room in the afternoon — a wooden shelf on the wall, a wooden floor, warm afternoon light — the {INPUT_1_AGE} person from the input image stands, one hand resting over their heart in relief, with a happy smile. Nearby, {GENDER_F_AGE_35_PARENT} rests a hand warmly on their shoulder. A fresh flower sits in a new cup on the wooden shelf behind them. Make the interaction read physically: each person faces the other person or the shared object, with hands and gaze directed to one connected action. Eye-level medium-wide shot, photorealistic, cinematic photography style. {INPUT_IMAGE_IDENTITY}
gists: In a cozy afternoon living room with a wooden shelf holding a blue ceramic vase, the child stands alone on the floor mid-throw, having just tossed a red ball up toward the vase with the ball arcing through the air straight at it, playful.

In the living room, the wooden shelf is now bare and the blue vase lies smashed into jagged broken pieces on the floor while the child stands alone among them, hands flying up beside their cheeks in shock.
In the living room beside the jagged broken pieces of the smashed blue vase with the shelf bare above, the child stands alone, hand to cheek, looking down worriedly — unsure what to do.

In the living room, the child points down at the jagged broken pieces of the smashed blue vase with an honest, nervous look while the mother kneels to their level, listening calmly — the child owns up.

In the living room, the child kneels using a small broom to sweep the jagged broken pieces of the smashed blue vase into a dustpan while the mother kneels beside them holding the dustpan steady, smiling gently — telling the truth leads to cleaning up safely together.
In the living room with a fresh flower in a new cup on the shelf, the child rests a hand over their heart in relief, happy, while the mother rests a hand on their shoulder — the truth feels lighter.
texts: {NAME} was bouncing a red ball in the living room. Boing, boing! "Catch!" laughed {NAME}. What fun!
Then — crash! The blue vase fell and broke into pieces. "Oh no!" gasped {NAME}, both hands flying up.
{NAME} looked at all the broken pieces and felt worried inside. "What should I do?" {NAME} whispered.
{NAME} found Mum and pointed to the vase. "I broke it," said {NAME}. "I'm sorry — it was an accident."

"That was brave and honest," said Mum gently, setting down a dustpan. "The pieces are sharp, so let's use the little broom." "I'll sweep carefully," said {NAME}. "And I'll hold the dustpan steady," said Mum.
Mum gave {NAME}'s shoulder a warm pat. "I feel better now," said {NAME}, smiling. The truth had made everything feel light inside.
